coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of dragonfruit-based silicon nanoparticles at room temperature and their application in the real-time fiber-optic detection of doxorubicin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

Liu, Shun

coredata.dc:description: Doxorubicin (DOX) has been extensively used in cancer chemotherapy as an antibiotic drug, but when DOX concentration exceeds the threshold value, irreversible liver toxicity and cardiotoxicity can be triggered. Therefore, it is a significant theme to detect DOX concentration of patient blood and metabolites for the optimization of drug dosage. Linear correlation between fluorescence quenching of silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) and DOX concentration enables SiNPs to present good prospect to address this tricky issue. Inspired by these aspects, we put forward a novel method to synthesize dragonfruit-based SiNPs (DSiNPs) at room temperature, and leverage them in the fiber-optic sensing of urine DOX. Firstly, we investigate a simple and green synthesis method of DSiNPs emitting blue-green fluorescence through a one-step redox reaction at room temperature with organosilane as silicon source and fresh dragonfruit juice as reducing agent. Secondly, depending on optical characterization, we demonstrate mechanism of DSiNPs in the detection of DOX. Thirdly, a fiber-optic sensor is used to measure the fluorescence intensity of DSiNPs under diverse salt concentrations, pH values, and illumination time to explore the tolerance of DSiNPs. Fourthly, based on optimization of parameters and validation of specificity, we acquire linear response range of this method to DOX from 0 to 50 μM with detection limit of 3 μM. Finally, this method is leveraged in the detection of urine DOX, and obtained the recovery rates of 92.30 % and 107.30 %. This method holds excellent potential in the clinical analysis of anti-tumor drugs for the advantages of environmental friendliness and simple operation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fiber-optic sensor
Silicon nanoparticles
Doxorubicin
Dragonfruit juice
Ratiometric ﬂuorescence

Influence of the interfering substances on the detection
Detection of urine DOX with synthesized DSiNPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagrams demonstrating the synthesis of DSiNPs for detection of DOX. (a) Cutting of dragonfruit. (b) Squashing of dragonfruit flesh. (c) Filtering of dragonfruit juice. (d) Centrifugation of dragonfruit juice. (e) Extraction of pure dragonfruit juice. (f) Addition of DAMO into the dragonfruit juice. (g) Mixing of DAMO and dragonfruit juice for the synthesis of DSiNPs. (h) Dilution of DSiNPs for detection of urine DOX.
Optical characteristics of DSiNPs. (a) TEM image of prepared DSiNPs. (b) EDS spectrum. (c) XRD pattern assessment of DSiNPs. (d) Spectrum of DSiNPs and DOX.
Characterization of synthesized DSiNPs. (a) FTIR spectrum. (b) Full range, (c) C1s, (d) N1s, (e) O1s, and (f) Si2p of XPS spectrum.
Detection principle of DOX concentration with DSiNPs. (a) Lifetime of DSiNPs (i) in 25 ns and (ii) in 200 ns. (b) Schematic demonstrating the detection principle.
Diagram of the experimental system for the detection of DOX with DSiNPs.

Fluorescence tolerance of DSiNPs. (a) Fluorescence intensity of DSiNPs in diverse-concentration NaCl solution. (b) Fluorescence intensity of DSiNPs at different time under consistent UV light. (c) Fluorescence intensity of DSiNPs under diverse pH values.
Parametric effects on the detection. (a and b) R f of detection system with increasing pH values and reaction time. (c) Fluorescence responses of DSiNPs to the urine contents.
Plot demonstrating relationship between R f value and DOX concentration.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of dragonfruit-based silicon nanoparticles at room temperature and their application in the real-time fiber-optic detection of doxorubicin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Control Engineering, Northeastern University at Qinhuangdao, Qinhuangdao 066004, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Control Engineering, Northeastern University at Qinhuangdao, Qinhuangdao 066004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Control Engineering, Northeastern University at Qinhuangdao

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doxorubicin (DOX) has been extensively used in cancer chemotherapy as an antibiotic drug, but when DOX concentration exceeds the threshold value, irreversible liver toxicity and cardiotoxicity can be triggered. Therefore, it is a significant theme to detect DOX concentration of patient blood and metabolites for the optimization of drug dosage. Linear correlation between fluorescence quenching of silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) and DOX concentration enables SiNPs to present good prospect to address this tricky issue. Inspired by these aspects, we put forward a novel method to synthesize dragonfruit-based SiNPs (DSiNPs) at room temperature, and leverage them in the fiber-optic sensing of urine DOX. Firstly, we investigate a simple and green synthesis method of DSiNPs emitting blue-green fluorescence through a one-step redox reaction at room temperature with organosilane as silicon source and fresh dragonfruit juice as reducing agent. Secondly, depending on optical characterization, we demonstrate mechanism of DSiNPs in the detection of DOX. Thirdly, a fiber-optic sensor is used to measure the fluorescence intensity of DSiNPs under diverse salt concentrations, pH values, and illumination time to explore the tolerance of DSiNPs. Fourthly, based on optimization of parameters and validation of specificity, we acquire linear response range of this method to DOX from 0 to 50 μM with detection limit of 3 μM. Finally, this method is leveraged in the detection of urine DOX, and obtained the recovery rates of 92.30 % and 107.30 %. This method holds excellent potential in the clinical analysis of anti-tumor drugs for the advantages of environmental friendliness and simple operation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a common anthracycline antibiotic, doxorubicin (DOX) has been recognized as an effective clinical chemotherapy drug [1–3], and extensively used in several types of human cancers, such as breast cancer [2], acute leukemia [4,5], malignant tumors [2,5], childhood solid tumors [6] and bronchogenic carcinomas [4]. However, different patients present various curative effects under the same drug dosage [5,7]. Meanwhile, when DOX concentration in human blood exceeds the threshold value, seriously irreversible health complications may be triggered in the human body, involving liver toxicity [8], cardiotoxicity [8,9], bone marrow suppression [5,8], and cardiomyopathy [6,9]. Therefore, accurate real-time determination of DOX concentration in human blood and metabolites during chemotherapy is of great significance for patients to achieve optimal treatment of cancers and minimize the toxicity to the patients [5,8,9].

To accomplish the detection of DOX concentration, various techniques have been developed [5,6,9], including liquid chromatography [5,9], microchip capillary electrophoresis [5], voltammetric [10,11], and chemiluminescence[5,12]. Although these methods can realize effective detection of DOX concentrations, they still present limitations for their distinct features, such as high cost, strict conditions, and complicated operations [6,13,14]. A large number of literatures have pointed out that the fluorescence analysis method based on fluorescent nanomaterials is a useful tool in the chemical, biological, and medical sciences for the merits of simple operation, strong specificity, and high efficiency and sensitivity [15–17]. Fluorescent nanomaterials have been widely used in environmental detection [18–20], biomedical engineering [16,21,22], and optoelectronic devices [16,18,23] for the structure-induced unique properties, such as surface effects and quantum size effects [24,25]. With zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterials becoming increasingly popular, silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) have been widely used in bioimaging [26–28], environmental monitoring [29], and biochemical analysis [30] for the advantages of excellent optical property [31–34], high biocompatibility [35], good water solubility [36,37] and biodegradability [38,39]. Noticeably, fluorescence light of SiNPs can be emitted well under the work parameters of DOX in the blood and urine. Therefore, SiNPs hold good potential in the detection of DOX concentration for the drug safety of patients in cancer treatment [40].

Current techniques make them unable to meet the requirements of the real-time detection [6,41]. Optical fiber can take on the transmission task of excitation light to the samples and the emitted fluorescence to the detector. As a result, fiber-optic sensors can accomplish sample excitation and emission collection by combining with sensing system in a simpler and more compact configuration. Moreover, due to the outstanding advantages of high sensitivity, lightweight structure, and chemical inertness, fiber-optic sensors can achieve effective detection of fluorescence intensity of SiNPs [42] and further realize real-time monitoring of DOX concentration for optimization of anti-tumor drug dosage. Therefore, the fiber-optic sensing method is promising in the detection of the DOX concentration with SiNPs.

In this work, we put forward a green and simple method to synthesize SiNPs with dragonfruit juice at room temperature and leveraged them in the fiber-optic detection of urine DOX. First, we investigated a novel method to synthesize dragonfruit-based SiNPs (DSiNPs) emitting blue-green fluorescence through a redox reaction at room temperature. Depending on the optical characterization of DSiNPs, we developed a novel fluorescence detection method of DOX based on the quenching effect of DOX on fluorescent DSiNPs. Besides, fluorescence quenching of DSiNPs presented a proportional response to the concentration of DOX, which enables this method to realize quantitative analysis of DOX in urine. Afterward, we studied the detection performance of this method under various work parameters, such as the pH value, detection time and interferent, and obtained the optimal detection conditions for the detection of DOX concentration. Finally, we leveraged this detection method to detect the DOX concentration of urine samples. This detection method holds good potential in the clinical analysis of the efficacy of anti-tumor drugs due to the merits of environmental friendliness, simple operation, and good specificity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of DSiNPs at room temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh dragonfruits were brought from the market in Qinhuangdao, China, and preserved in the refrigerator at T=4 °C. 3-(2-Aminoethylamino) propyltriethoxysilane (DAMO), doxorubicin, and sodium chloride were purchased from Aladdin. DI water was prepared using a water purification system (HUIYIPU HYP-QX-UP).

The synthesis process of DSiNPs is schematically presented in Fig. 1. We chopped the flesh of a fresh dragonfruit with the weight of about 500 g into small pieces (Fig. 1a). Small pieces of dragonfruit were put into the mortar and grated using the pestle to obtain the juice (Fig. 1b). Left solid dragonfruit flesh was filtered from squeezed rude dragonfruit juice using the filtering net (Fig. 1c). Processed dragonfruit juice was put into the tube to conduct the centrifugal treatment at the rotation speed of 4000 rpm for 8 min using a centrifuge (Shanghai Anting Scientific Instrument Factory TGL-16B) to screen residual small-size flesh (Fig. 1d and e).

Dragonfruit juice and organosilane with a volume ratio of 5:1 were infused in a 20 mL beaker (Fig. 1f), and we used the magnetic stirrer to mix fully these two fluids (Fig. 1g) for 20 min to synthesize DSiNPs at room temperature (25°C). The reaction process of synthesizing DSiNPs is shown in Figure S1. We selected DAMO as the saline for its outstanding advantages of low-cost and biosafety. In the reaction, DAMO would serve as a silicon source and provide the functional group at room temperature, while anthocyanins in dragon fruit juice would serve as a reducing agent. Afterwards, synthesized DSiNPs were dialyzed through a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da against ultrapure water for 8 h. The dialysis membrane was preprocessed by soaking in DI water at 100 °C for 20 min. Finally, synthesized DSiNPs were stored in the refrigerator at T=4 °C. DSiNPs stock solution needed to be diluted 50 times with DI water before detection of the urine DOX concentration (Fig. 1h).

After synthesis of DSiNPs, we characterized their morphology and optical properties. The morphology of fluorescent DSiNPs and Si element analysis were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM-F200, Japan) ( Fig. 2a). According to Fig. 2a, spherical DSiNPs have good dispersion with a diameter of about 3–5 nm, which can contribute to the blue-green photoluminescence of DSiNPs. Besides, a particle analysis using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (JED-2300 Analysis Station Plus, Japan) exhibited a significant peak at 1.739 KeV, validating the existence of Si element and the production is really SiNPs (Fig. 2b).

We have also investigated the silicon signal by using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Rigaku SmartLab SE, Japan). The synthesized DSiNPs showed a broad band of signal with a peak at 21.66° (Fig. 2c). It can be seen that silicon element in DSiNPs mainly exist in the form of amorphous SiO2, consistent with previous literature [31]. The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of DSiNPs was measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA FluoroMax, USA). The obtained PLQY of DSiNPs was 70 % (Figure S2). The optical properties of fluorescent DSiNPs were characterized by UV–visible spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 2d describes UV–visible absorption and emission spectrum of the DSiNPs. Emission wavelength of the DSiNPs is approximately 494 nm, which can effectively overlap with the absorption spectrum (about 490 nm) of DOX. Theoretically, DSiNPs can accomplish detection of DOX concentration based on the inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer.

Then, we used the method of FTIR spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20, USA) as presented in Fig. 3a to investigate the functional groups attached on the surface of DSiNPs. An obvious stretching vibration absorption peak of N-H occurred at the wave number of 3432 cm−1.[32] The absorption peaks which correspond to the stretching and bend vibrations of unsaturated C-H appeared at the wave numbers of 2924 and 1456 cm−1 [32,34]. The bands at the wavenumber of 1128 cm−1 and 1042 cm−1 were referred to the Si-O-Si stretching vibration [32,34]. A secondary amine included the band at the wavenumber of 691 cm−1 [32,39]. According to these FTIR data, we can validate the existence of (2-aminoethylamino)propyl on the surface of DSiNPs. These functional groups play a crucial part in the water dispersibility, stability, and blue-green photoluminescence of synthesized DSiNPs.

We further investigated the functional groups on the surfaces of synthesized DsiNPs using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA), and the results are presented in Fig. 3b-f. According to Fig. 3b, it can be observed that there are mainly five peaks with the binding energy of 101.1, 153.1, 285.1, 399.1, and 531.1 eV in the full-scan survey, which refers to the elements of C, N, O and Si in the DSiNPs [42,43]. Carbon atoms in DSiNPs exist in five different states, namely C-Si (284.0 eV), C-C (284.8 eV), C-N (285.6 eV), C-O (286.4 eV) and CO (287.7 eV) [13,39]. Three peaks at the binding energy of 400.6 eV, 399.3 eV and 398.2 eV describe three states of nitrogen atoms in the DSiNPs, Si-N, C-N, and C-(N)3.[13,44] The spectra of O1s consists of three peaks at the binding energy of 530.4, 531.6, and 533.1 eV [13,35], corresponding to three types of oxygen atoms, CO, C-O and Si-O. Silicon atom exists in the DSiNPs in the forms of Si-N, Si-C and Si-O with the binding energy of 100.9 eV, 101.8 eV, and 103.0 eV, respectively [35,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection principle of DOX with synthesized DSiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: DSiNPs possess unique optical properties and excellent biocompatibility, enabling them to have broad application prospects in biomedicine. Because lower-toxicity DSiNPs don’t contain heavy metal cadmium, they can interact with DOX in a safer and more reliable manner. As the emission spectrum (494 nm) of fluorescent DSiNPs effectively overlaps with the absorption spectrum (490 nm) of DOX, DOX will produce an IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer on fluorescent DSiNPs, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of DSiNPs. The fluorescence quenching degree of DSiNPs is linearly correlated with the concentration of DOX within a certain range. DOX can produce a fluorescence emission peak at the wavelength of 597 nm, we can use the ratiometric ﬂuorescence (R f=I 597/I 490) to describe the quenching degree of fluorescent DSiNPs for accurate quantitative analysis of DOX concentration of human urine.

To further confirm the detection principle of DOX with DSiNPs, we tested the fluorescence lifetime of DSiNPs, as shown in Fig. 4a. Under the excitation of 400 nm, DSiNPs and DOX showed a strong emission spectrum at the wavelength of 560 nm and 610 nm. Moreover, the absorption spectrum of the mixture of DSiNPs and DOX reached the peak value at the wavelength of 480 nm (Fig. 2b). Therefore, the mechanism of quenching DSiNPs with DOX could be fluorescence resonance energy transfer or IFE. To further demonstrate the detection principle, we investigate the fluorescence lifetime of DSiNPs. As shown in Fig. 4a, the lifetime of DSiNPs was approximately 6.77 ns. Then, we added DOX into the DSiNPs and tested the lifetime again, which was about 6.80 ns. DOX didn’t have an effect on the fluorescence lifetime of DSiNPs. Therefore, the mechanism for sensing DOX using DSiNPs is IFE (Fig. 4b). This detection method can effectively detect the concentration of DOX in human urine and realize real-time monitoring of the patient’s status under the action of anti-cancer drugs.

The experimental platform used in this work consisted of a laser light source (375 nm), a Y-shaped fiber coupler, a long-wave pass filter (<400 nm), an ocean spectrometer (OCEAN OPTICS Maya, 2000PRO), and a computer as shown in Fig. 5. The excitation light emitted by the laser source reached the fiber probe through the fiber coupler and excited the fluorescent DSiNPs in contact with the fiber tip to emit green fluorescence. At the same time, the optical fiber probe collecting the fluorescence emitted by DSiNPs was collected by an optical spectrum analyzer through a filter. The filter was mainly used to eliminate the interference of stray light and excitation light to improve the quality of collected emission spectra. We can use the computer to process the data collected by the optical spectrum analyzer and control the detection system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tolerance of DSiNPs to NaCl concentration
ce:section.ce:para: NaCl solutions with concentrations of 0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mM were prepared to investigate the salt-tolerance stability of fabricated DSiNPs. 100 μL DSiNPs solution, 100 μL DI water, and 100 μL NaCl solutions with diverse concentrations were mixed evenly, and then we used the fiber-optic sensor to detect the change in fluorescence intensity. Besides, spectrums were observed and recorded with the optical spectrum analyzer. Measured fluorescence intensities of DSiNPs under different NaCl concentrations are shown in Fig. 6a and b. When the NaCl concentration was increased from 0 to 100 mM, the fluorescence intensity of DSiNPs didn’t present evident fluctuation. Therefore, synthesized fluorescent DSiNPs present excellent tolerance to NaCl concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tolerance of DSiNPs to illumination time
ce:section.ce:para: We mixed 100 μL DSiNPs solution and 200 μL DI water to study the light stability of DSiNPs under consistent UV light. In the process of the test, a fiber-optic sensor was put in the mixture to detect the change of fluorescence intensity every 5 min within 1 h, and an optical spectrum analyzer was used to observe and record the experimental data. The fluorescence intensity of DSiNPs at different times under consistent UV light is presented in Fig. 6b. As the illumination time increased, the fluorescence intensity of DSiNPs witnessed a slight decrease, and the total decrease of the fluorescence intensity was less than 18.7 % from t=0–60 min. Therefore, synthesized DSiNPs present good light stability under consistent light within 1 hour.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tolerance of DSiNPs to pH value
ce:section.ce:para: We also investigated the fluorescence response of DSiNPs to the environmental pH value by mixing 100 μL DSiNPs and 100 μL BR buffer solutions with different pH values. Fluorescence intensity was collected with an ocean spectrometer under different pH values, as presented in Fig. 6c. With an increase in the pH value of the solution from 2 to 6, the fluorescence intensity of DSiNPs witnessed a sharp decrease. Moreover, when pH>6, the fluorescence intensity of DSiNPs nearly reached a steady state with a slight fluctuation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DOX concentration with DSiNPs

ce:section.ce:para: Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution is a mixture of 0.04 M phosphoric acid, 0.04 M boric acid, and 0.04 M acetic acid. BR buffer solutions with different pH values (2−12) were adjusted by sodium hydroxide solution (0.2 M concentration) and measured with a pH meter. 100 μL DSiNPs solution, 100 μL BR buffer solution, 100 μL urine sample, and 100 μL DOX solution or sample solution were mixed evenly. A fiber-optic sensor connecting with an optical spectrum analyzer was used to measure and record the fluorescence intensity of DOX samples. In order to obtain the optimal detection results, we investigated the influences of conditions, such as pH value, duration time, and possible interfering urine substances on the detection performance of this method. Depending on this, we gained optimal parameters for the quantitative detection of DOX concentration in human urine using DSiNPs. Because DOX has a fluorescence emission peak at the wavelength of 597 nm (I 597), DOX concentration can be quantitatively analyzed based on the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of DOX to that of DSiNPs (I 490) (R f =I 597/I 490).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the pH value on the detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to optimize the pH value of the detection solution, 100 μL DSiNPs solution and 100 μL DOX solution (25 μM) were adjusted with 100 μL different-pH BR buffer solution evenly. After 30 min, the fluorescence intensity of DSiNPs at different pH values was detected. The change curves related to the recorded data are shown in Fig. 7a. With increasing the pH value from 2 to 4, R f witnessed a consistent increase trend. When pH value of detection solution exceeded 4, R f value began to present a gradual decrease trend. Therefore, pH value of the BR buffer solution should be set to 4 in the detection of urine DOX concentration with DSiNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the duration time on the detection
ce:section.ce:para: After determining the optimal pH value, 100 μL BR buffer solution (pH=4), 100 μL DSiNPs, and 100 μL DOX solution (25 μM) were mixed to measure the fluorescence intensity with the spectrometer at t=0, t=5, t=10, t=20, t=30, and t=40 min. According to Fig. 7b, R f exhibited a consistent increase from t=0 min to t=30 min. It is worth noting that R f reached a steady state when t>30 min. Therefore, we can obtain excellent detection results at t>30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the interfering substances on the detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal detection conditions, the influences of possible interfering substances in urine on the detection of DOX concentration were explored. Possible interfering substances in urine mainly include biomolecules and metal ions. In detail, biomolecules include glycine (Cly), cysteine (Cys), serine (Ser), glutamic acid (Glu), and lysine (Lys). and metal ions mainly include calcium ions, zinc ions, copper ions, iron ions, sodium ions, and potassium ions. Biomolecules and metal ions for investigation of interferent effects on the detection of DOX were brought from Sigma Aldrich (USA). 100 μL DSiNPs, 100 μL BR buffer solution (pH=4), and 100 μL the solution containing the above interfering substance at the concentration of 250 μM. At t>30 min, the fluorescence intensity of different interfering substances was measured using the fiber-optic sensor, which is presented in Fig. 7c. Except for DOX, DSiNPs have no evident response to other interfering substances, even though the concentration of interferents (250 μM) far exceeded that of DOX (25 μM). Therefore, this method possesses good selection specificity for DOX and holds good prospects in the detection and analysis of biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of urine DOX with synthesized DSiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: 100 μL DSiNPs, 100 μL BR buffer solution (pH=4), and 100 μL different concentrations of DOX were mixed evenly. After 30 min, we used an ocean spectrometer to detect and record the fluorescence intensity of the detection system with DOX concentration from 0 to 50 μM. The fluorescence intensities at the wavelength of 490 nm and 597 nm of each group were detected to calculate the R f value. The detection results are shown in Fig. 8. We performed linear fitting on the detected R f and DOX concentrations. The relationship curve between R f value and DOX concentration can be regarded as the standard curve. When DOX concentration is in the range of 0–50 μM, R f has a linear relationship with the DOX concentration, which is, y=0.037x+0.599 (R 2=0.991), and the detection limit is 3 μM. 10 μM and 20 μM DOX were added to two urine samples, and their fluorescence intensities were measured using fiber-optic sensors, as given in Table 1. We divided the recovered amount by the added amount and multiplied by 100 % to calculate the recovery rates. From Table 1, recovery rates of DOX in the urine samples were 92.30 % and 107.30 %, which demonstrates the accuracy and reliability of this method in the detection of DOX in actual urine samples.

In this work, we put forward a novel synthesis method of DSiNPs at room temperature and investigated their performance in the fiber-optic sensing of urine DOX based on IFE. Firstly, we synthesized DSiNPs emitting blue-green fluorescence at room temperature through a redox reaction with organosilane as a silicon source and fresh dragonfruit juice as a reducing agent. Synthesized DSiNPs hold the advantages of environmental friendliness, water solubility, and good stability. Secondly, we investigated the influences of salt concentration, pH value, and illumination time on the fluorescence intensity of prepared DSiNPs. Thirdly, the parameters affecting the detection of DOX by fluorescent DSiNPs were explored. When the pH value of the detection system was about 4 and the reaction time exceeded 30 min, the detection results were optimal. Moreover, the detection specificity of DSiNPs to DOX was validated by investigating the influence of biomolecules and metal ions existing in urine samples on the detection of DOX. Fourthly, under the optimal detection parameters, the linear range of the method for detecting DOX was 0–50 μM with a detection limit of 3 μM. Finally, the method successfully realized the determination of DOX in actual urine and can be used for the analysis and detection of DOX in clinical urine samples with a recovery rate of 92.30 % and 107.30 %. This method holds good potential to be an alternative in the clinical diagnosis and biochemical analysis for environmental friendliness and simple operation.

Jingkang Li: Writing – review & editing. Chungang Wu: Writing – review & editing. Yueti Li: Writing – review & editing. Mo Shen: Writing – review & editing. Shun Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation. Sudu Bilige: Writing – review & editing. Zhipeng Song: Writing – review & editing. Jishun Shi: Writing – review & editing. Liangliang Sun: Writing – review & editing. Yong Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Xiaoming Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Zihao Chen: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 62304034], the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province [grant numbers A2022501004, B2022501009, F2023501006, F2023501008], the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities [grant number N2423026], the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation [2024M750713].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.135174.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An aptamer-based biosensor for detection of doxorubicin by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
